steps.title: Identify where the descriptive clause must go
steps.content: The key signal is the clause "which are skeletal muscles." When a clause describes a noun and is not essential to the main meaning of the sentence, it must appear right after that noun and be enclosed by commas on both sides.

steps.title: Apply the rule to the choices
steps.content:
brachialis, which are skeletal muscles, are cylindrical in shape and help places the clause immediately after the subject with commas on both sides, then continues with the main predicates "are cylindrical in shape and help move the foot and forearm, respectively." ✓

  • "brachialis are cylindrical in shape, which are skeletal muscles, and help": Places "which are skeletal muscles" after "cylindrical in shape" rather than after the noun it modifies, separating the clause from the noun it describes. A stray comma after "help" further breaks the sentence. ✗
  • "brachialis are cylindrical in shape and help, which are skeletal muscles,": Inserts the clause after "help," placing it far from the noun it modifies and breaking up the verb phrase "help move the foot and forearm." ✗
  • "brachialis are cylindrical, which are skeletal muscles, in shape and help": Inserts the clause inside the phrase "cylindrical in shape," splitting that phrase apart rather than following the noun with a properly paired comma clause. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
